Feedback systems — Part 2
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image By AuthorLast time we established something very fascinating… You’ve been running control loops your whole life without even realizing it…. Shower knobs, bike balance, thermostat e.t.c…Sense, Compare, Act, Repeats…
But what part 1 left out is: knowing you’re wrong isn’t the hard bit… Deciding how much or hard to correct is the interesting part…. And where most people and machinery mess up..
It turns out everytime you are correcting anything, you’re actually running three different Instincts at once…. They have never just been named…
Instinct One: React to the size of the mistake.
Back to the shower example again;
If the water from the shower is freezing, you crank the hot water knob hard. If it’s just slightly cool, you don’t twist the knob violently, that would be crazy 😅…. The size of your reaction naturally scales with the size of the problem.
Now let me confuse you a bit 🥲…. This is the most obvious instinct but the one that quietly fails on its own. Here’s why; As the error becomes smaller, so does your correction, which sounds right, until the correction gets so small it’s basically nothing, and you’re left sitting slightly off target, forever, because the error is “small enough” that your instinct stops bothering to fix it.
Instinct Two: Remember If it keeps happening.
Now here’s the fix your brain does automatically without you being told to: If a small error just won’t go away no matter how many times you’ve nudged, you start correcting harder than the error requires, because you’re not just reacting to right now, you’re to the fact that it’s been wrong for a while ….
Think about pushing a shopping cart ( or trolley as it called in some regions) with one slightly stuck wheel… at first you correct gently, matching the small pull… But if that same pull keeps happening as you keep pushing, you don’t give it the same tiny correction, you start pushing harder than the required force because your body has started accounting for the fact that this isn’t a one time thing, it’s a pattern..
Instinct Three: Brace against sudden changes.
Now the trickiest one. Imagine you’re carrying a full glass of water and someone bumps into you… Your instinct isn’t just to react to how tilted the glass is but how fast it’s tilting too…. A sudden violent push gets an immediate, sharp counter move, even before any water has been spelt.
This is sort of prediction, not of the future but of where things are headed based on how quickly they’re changing. It’s an instinct that keeps you for overcorrecting into a worse mess than you started with….
Put Together, these three Instincts are basically all of control theory.
Formally, these three Instincts have names, and you’ll see them again later: Proportional (React to the size of the error), Integral (React to how long it’s been wrong), Derivative (React to how fast it’s changing). Together engineers call this combination PID control and it shows up in nearly every real feedback system.
But notice that this isn’t new.. its just naming three things your body already does everytime you correct anything.. The only difference between you and a robot or machine is that you do all three Instinctively, instantly without thinking. A robot has to be told explicitly, exactly how much weight to give each one…
Now here’s the twist part 3 will dig into properly: Getting the balance between these three Instincts right is genuinely difficulty… Lean more on one, and you find yourself in a mess…
I hope This was insightful… I would like to hear your thoughts on this topic, so kindly leave a comment…
Further Reading:

• Brian Douglas — Control Systems Lectures (YouTube): covers exactly this PID breakdown visually.

• MIT OpenCourseWare — 6.302: Feedback Systems: for anyone who wants the formal version of what’s in this article.
